definition: An indicator of dissolved material in fruit juice, mostly sugars. It is especially important in processing tomatoes because higher values reduce the energy needed to reach the required concentration.
A carotenoid pigment largely responsible for the red color of tomatoes and an important quality indicator for processing fruit.
The portion of harvested produce that meets market or factory specifications after infested, rotten, or non-compliant fruit is excluded.
The quantity of microorganisms present on or within raw material. Wounds, tunnels, and rot may increase it and compromise processing quality and safety.
The continuous presence of suitable host plants between seasons, allowing a pest to survive and move without a complete break in its life cycle.
A plant on which a pest can feed, reproduce, or complete an important part of its life cycle.
A plant other than the principal crop on which a pest can survive or reproduce, potentially becoming a source of field infestation.
A plant family that includes tomato, potato, eggplant, pepper, and wild species that may serve as hosts for Tuta absoluta.
A pest that has moved beyond its native range, established, spread, and caused agricultural or economic effects in new regions.
The pest level at which action should be taken to prevent expected damage from exceeding acceptable loss and control cost.
A life cycle with four distinct stages: egg, larva, pupa, and adult.
A developmental period between two larval molts. Tissue consumption generally increases as the larva advances through its instars.
The pupa is a non-feeding stage in which the larva transforms into an adult; pupation is the process of entering this stage.
Areas or tunnels formed as a larva feeds between plant-tissue layers. They may first appear translucent, then turn brown and dry.
The internal leaf tissue between the upper and lower epidermis that contains most photosynthetic cells.
Tuta absoluta belongs to the order Lepidoptera and the family Gelechiidae, which contains many small mining moths.
A newer taxonomic combination used by some references for the same pest widely known as Tuta absoluta—not a different pest.
A decision-based approach combining monitoring and cultural, biological, behavioral, and chemical measures for effective control with the lowest practical economic and environmental impact.
A pheromone is a chemical communication signal. Synthetic lures in traps monitor male activity, but they do not directly measure larvae inside plants.
The component responsible for a pesticide’s biological effect. The same active ingredient may be sold under different trade names.
The biological process through which a pesticide disrupts a specific function in the pest, such as nerve signaling, energy production, or growth.
A number or code classifying insecticides by mode of action. It supports group rotation to reduce resistance selection pressure rather than rating product strength.
A heritable increase in a pest population’s ability to survive a treatment that was previously effective and pass those traits to later generations.
The PHI is the required time between the last application and harvest; an MRL is the highest legally permitted residue level in food when an approved use is followed.
Beneficial organisms reduce pest numbers through predation or parasitism. Trichogramma species attack pest eggs and prevent larvae from emerging.
A fungal or bacterial infection entering through a wound or hole made by the pest, increasing tissue breakdown and potentially causing complete fruit loss.
Active infestation shows a live larva, fresh feeding, or an expanding mine; old infestation is dry and inactive and may no longer contain a larva.
A former or alternative scientific name for the same species following taxonomic revision or transfer between genera; it does not indicate a different pest.
A level in the classification hierarchy, such as kingdom, phylum, class, order, family, genus, and species.
Small flies whose larvae mine leaves. They belong to Diptera and differ taxonomically and morphologically from the lepidopteran Tuta absoluta.
The entry, establishment, and spread of an organism beyond its former range, potentially causing major economic or ecological effects.
Regulatory measures that limit pest entry or movement through seedlings, fruits, containers, transport, and other plant materials.
A pest's ability to survive, reproduce, and maintain a continuing population in a new area without repeated introductions.
Complete elimination of a pest population from a defined area. It is most feasible when a small isolated focus is detected early.
Natural dispersal moves adults between nearby sites; human-assisted dispersal carries the pest farther through trade, seedlings, fruits, and containers.
A programmed developmental arrest that helps some insects survive unfavorable conditions. Tuta absoluta does not require it when hosts and suitable temperatures remain available.
A standardized identifier assigned by the European and Mediterranean Plant Protection Organization for exchanging taxonomic and distribution data.
The study of an organism's external form and structure, including size, color, body divisions, appendages, wings, and diagnostic markings.
The hardened covering of a larval head that bears the mouthparts, simple eyes, and short antennae; it supports identification and larval-instar assessment.
A hardened plate on the first thoracic segment behind the head and an important morphological feature for distinguishing larvae.
Solid waste produced during larval feeding. Its dark accumulation inside a mine can help identify the infestation pattern.
Detailed morphological traits used by specialists to confirm species identity and distinguish closely resembling species.
Confirmation of species identity using specialist diagnostic, microscopic, or molecular methods when field examination alone is insufficient.
